keyConcepts.label: Expanding a rational to a continued fraction
keyConcepts.microLesson: Every rational $p/q$ has a finite continued fraction $[a_0; a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n]$, meaning $a_0 + \cfrac{1}{a_1 + \cfrac{1}{a_2 + \cdots}}$. The terms are exactly the quotients from the Euclidean algorithm on $(p, q)$. Example: $\frac{29}{12}$: $29 = 2\cdot12 + 5$, $12 = 2\cdot5 + 2$, $5 = 2\cdot2 + 1$, $2 = 2\cdot1$, so $\frac{29}{12} = [2; 2, 2, 2]$. Read off the quotients $2, 2, 2, 2$ in order.

keyConcepts.label: Convergents $p_k/q_k$
keyConcepts.microLesson: Truncating a continued fraction at term $a_k$ gives the $k$-th CONVERGENT $p_k/q_k$, the best rational approximation with denominator $\le q_k$. Build them with the recurrence $p_k = a_k p_{k-1} + p_{k-2}$ and $q_k = a_k q_{k-1} + q_{k-2}$, seeded by $p_{-1}=1, q_{-1}=0$ (so $p_0 = a_0, q_0 = 1$). For $[3; 7, 15, 1]$ (from $\pi$): $\frac{3}{1}, \frac{22}{7}, \frac{333}{106}, \frac{355}{113}$ — the famous approximations of $\pi$.

keyConcepts.label: Evaluating a continued fraction
keyConcepts.microLesson: To turn $[a_0; a_1, \dots, a_n]$ back into a fraction $p/q$, fold from the INNERMOST term outward: start with $a_n$, then repeatedly take the reciprocal and add the next term. Example: $[1; 2, 3] = 1 + \cfrac{1}{2 + \frac{1}{3}} = 1 + \cfrac{1}{7/3} = 1 + \frac{3}{7} = \frac{10}{7}$. Each step inverts and adds; a couple of careful fraction additions recover the exact value.

keyConcepts.label: Best rational approximation
keyConcepts.microLesson: Convergents are the BEST rational approximations: no fraction with a smaller denominator is closer to the value. The error after the $k$-th convergent is tiny, $\left|x - \frac{p_k}{q_k}\right| < \frac{1}{q_k q_{k+1}}$. This is why $\frac{22}{7}$ (error $\approx 0.00126$) and $\frac{355}{113}$ (error $\approx 0.00000027$) are the classic approximations of $\pi$ — a large next term $a_{k+1}$ signals an especially good convergent, since it makes $q_{k+1}$ jump.

lecture.body:
## The Euclidean algorithm, written as a tower

You already know the Euclidean algorithm computes $\gcd(p, q)$ by repeated division with remainder. A **continued fraction** is nothing more than those same divisions, stacked into a single expression. Every rational number $\frac{p}{q}$ can be written

$$\frac{p}{q} = a_0 + \cfrac{1}{a_1 + \cfrac{1}{a_2 + \cfrac{1}{\ddots + \cfrac{1}{a_n}}}} = [a_0; a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n],$$

where the $a_i$ are positive integers (with $a_0$ possibly zero or negative). The compact bracket notation $[a_0; a_1, \dots, a_n]$ — semicolon after the first term, commas after — is how we record it. These objects give the *best possible* rational approximations to any real number, which is why they run everything from calendar design to the gear ratios in mechanical clocks.

## Expanding a rational: it's just Euclid

To find the continued fraction of $\frac{p}{q}$, run the Euclidean algorithm and **collect the quotients**.

### Worked expansion: $\frac{67}{29}$

$$\begin{aligned}
67 &= \mathbf{2}\cdot 29 + 9 \\
29 &= \mathbf{3}\cdot 9 + 2 \\
9 &= \mathbf{4}\cdot 2 + 1 \\
2 &= \mathbf{2}\cdot 1 + 0.
\end{aligned}$$

The quotients, in order, are $2, 3, 4, 2$, so

$$\frac{67}{29} = [2; 3, 4, 2].$$

At each line the quotient is $a_k = \lfloor \text{current} \rfloor$, and the remainder feeds the reciprocal for the next line. The algorithm terminates exactly because the remainders strictly decrease — which is why *every* rational has a **finite** continued fraction, while irrationals get infinite ones.

## Convergents: the approximations along the way

Stop the tower early — say after $a_k$ — and evaluate what you have. That rational is the **$k$-th convergent** $\frac{p_k}{q_k}$. You do not need to collapse the tower each time; there is a clean recurrence. With the conventions $p_{-1} = 1, q_{-1} = 0, p_{-2} = 0, q_{-2} = 1$:

$$p_k = a_k\,p_{k-1} + p_{k-2}, \qquad q_k = a_k\,q_{k-1} + q_{k-2}.$$

### Worked convergents of $[2; 3, 4, 2]$

| $k$ | $a_k$ | $p_k = a_k p_{k-1} + p_{k-2}$ | $q_k = a_k q_{k-1} + q_{k-2}$ | $p_k/q_k$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 2 | $2$ | $1$ | $2$ |
| 1 | 3 | $3\cdot2+1 = 7$ | $3\cdot1+0 = 3$ | $7/3$ |
| 2 | 4 | $4\cdot7+2 = 30$ | $4\cdot3+1 = 13$ | $30/13$ |
| 3 | 2 | $2\cdot30+7 = 67$ | $2\cdot13+3 = 29$ | $67/29$ |

The last convergent returns the original $\frac{67}{29}$, as it must. A useful identity to check your arithmetic: consecutive convergents satisfy $p_k q_{k-1} - p_{k-1} q_k = (-1)^{k+1}$ — for instance $30\cdot3 - 7\cdot13 = 90 - 91 = -1$.

## Evaluating a continued fraction back to a fraction

Going the other way — from $[a_0; a_1, \dots, a_n]$ to $\frac{p}{q}$ — fold from the **innermost** term outward, at each step inverting and adding.

### Worked evaluation: $[2; 1, 3]$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{innermost: } & 1 + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{4}{3},\\
\text{then: } & 2 + \frac{1}{4/3} = 2 + \frac{3}{4} = \frac{11}{4}.
\end{aligned}$$

So $[2; 1, 3] = \frac{11}{4}$. (The convergent recurrence gives the same answer faster, but the fold makes the meaning transparent.)

## Why convergents are the *best* approximations

Here is the theorem that makes continued fractions special. The convergent $\frac{p_k}{q_k}$ is a **best rational approximation**: no fraction with denominator $\le q_k$ lies closer to $x$. And the error is controlled sharply:

$$\left| x - \frac{p_k}{q_k} \right| < \frac{1}{q_k\, q_{k+1}}.$$

The expansion of $\pi$ is $[3; 7, 15, 1, 292, \dots]$, whose convergents are

$$3,\quad \frac{22}{7},\quad \frac{333}{106},\quad \frac{355}{113},\quad \dots$$

The convergent $\frac{22}{7}$ is off by only $\approx 0.00126$; $\frac{355}{113}$ is off by $\approx 2.7\times10^{-7}$ — accurate enough for almost any engineering purpose, with a three-digit denominator. **A large next term is the signal of an unusually good convergent**: the $292$ after $\frac{355}{113}$ is why that fraction is so astonishingly close (the next $q$ jumps by a factor of 292).

### Pitfall

> The continued fraction of an *integer* is trivial ($[n]$, a single term), and you must never divide by a zero remainder mid-expansion — the algorithm stops the instant the remainder hits 0. When evaluating a fold, invert only nonzero partial values.

## Where continued fractions live

Beyond $\pi$, continued fractions solve Pell's equation $x^2 - Ny^2 = 1$ (the convergents of $\sqrt{N}$ hand you the solutions), they underlie the best-rational-approximation step in **Wiener's attack** on RSA with a small private exponent, they explain why the 12-tone musical scale and the Gregorian calendar's leap-year rule are the compromises they are, and they design the tooth counts of precision gear trains. The same four moves you practice here — expand, build convergents, evaluate, approximate — are exactly what those applications call.

application.summary: Continued fractions turn the Euclidean algorithm into the theory of best rational approximation. Their convergents are the closest fractions for a given denominator size, powering approximations of constants like $\pi$, solutions of Pell's equation, gear-train and calendar design, and Wiener's attack on weak RSA keys.
application.workedExample:
**Designing a gear ratio to approximate $\pi$.**

A planetarium builder needs two meshing gears whose tooth-count ratio approximates $\pi$ as closely as possible, but no gear can have more than ~120 teeth (bigger gears don't fit). Which ratio should they cut?

Expand $\pi = [3; 7, 15, 1, 292, \dots]$ and take convergents until the denominator would exceed the limit:

$$\frac{3}{1},\quad \frac{22}{7},\quad \frac{333}{106},\quad \frac{355}{113},\quad \dots$$

The convergent $\frac{355}{113}$ has a denominator of 113 — just under the 120-tooth cap — and approximates $\pi$ to within $2.7\times10^{-7}$. A 355-tooth driver meshed with a 113-tooth follower reproduces $\pi$ to seven decimal places; the next convergent $\frac{103993}{33102}$ would need impossibly large gears for a barely-better fit. Because convergents are provably the best approximations for their denominator size, the builder can be *certain* no ratio with $\le 113$ teeth on the follower does better than $\frac{355}{113}$ — the continued fraction hands them the optimal design directly, with a guarantee no trial-and-error search could provide. This is exactly how 17th- and 18th-century clockmakers cut astronomical gear trains, and how Wiener's RSA attack recovers a small secret exponent from the convergents of the public key.
domainNotes: **Approximation of constants:** convergents give the best rational approximations of $\pi, e, \sqrt{2}$; the huge partial quotient 292 in $\pi$ explains why $355/113$ is so uncannily accurate.
**Pell's equation:** solutions of $x^2 - Ny^2 = 1$ come directly from the convergents of the continued fraction of $\sqrt{N}$.
**Cryptography:** Wiener's attack recovers a small RSA private exponent $d$ by testing the convergents of the public fraction $e/n$ — a real reason to avoid small $d$.

**Engineering, music, calendars:** precision gear trains, the equal-tempered scale ($2^{7/12} \approx 3/2$), and the Gregorian leap-year rule are all best-approximation compromises continued fractions make explicit.

problems.prompt: Expand $\frac{7}{3}$ as a continued fraction $[a_0; a_1]$ using the Euclidean algorithm.

steps.instruction: The remainder is 1; invert $\frac{3}{1}$. What is $a_1 = \lfloor 3/1 \rfloor$?
steps.hint: $3/1 = 3$ exactly, so this is the last term.

problems.prompt: Expand $\frac{29}{12}$ as a continued fraction $[a_0; a_1, a_2, a_3]$. Report each term.
problems.solutionNarrative: The Euclidean quotients of $(29, 12)$ are $2, 2, 2, 2$, so $\frac{29}{12} = [2; 2, 2, 2]$.

problems.prompt: Expand $\frac{67}{29}$ as a continued fraction. Report all four terms $[a_0; a_1, a_2, a_3]$.
For the continued fraction $[3; 4, 2]$, compute the first two convergents $\frac{p_0}{q_0}$ and $\frac{p_1}{q_1}$.

steps.instruction: The zeroth convergent is $\frac{a_0}{1} = \frac{3}{1}$. Give it as a fraction.
The first convergent is $\frac{a_1 a_0 + 1}{a_1} = \frac{4\cdot3 + 1}{4}$. Give it as a fraction.

problems.prompt: For $[2; 3, 4]$, use the recurrence $p_k = a_k p_{k-1} + p_{k-2}$, $q_k = a_k q_{k-1} + q_{k-2}$ to find the convergent $\frac{p_2}{q_2}$.

steps.instruction: And $q_2 = 4\cdot3 + 1 = 13$. Give the convergent $\frac{p_2}{q_2}$ as a fraction.

problems.prompt: The continued fraction of $\pi$ starts $[3; 7, 15, 1]$. Compute its convergents to reach the famous $\frac{355}{113}$.
problems.solutionNarrative: The convergents are $\frac{3}{1}, \frac{22}{7}, \frac{333}{106}, \frac{355}{113}$; the last matches $\pi$ to seven decimals.

problems.prompt: Evaluate the continued fraction $[3; 4] = 3 + \frac{1}{4}$ as a single fraction.
Evaluate $[1; 2, 3]$ back to a fraction, folding from the innermost term.
Evaluate $[2; 1, 2, 3]$ to a single fraction, folding from the innermost term.
A value has continued fraction $[3; 5, 2]$. Its first convergent $\frac{p_1}{q_1}$ is already a good rational approximation. Give it.
The continued fraction of $\pi$ begins $[3; 7]$. Use this convergent as a rational approximation of $\pi$, and give the approximation error to 4 decimal places (use $\pi \approx 3.14159$).

steps.instruction: Its decimal value is $22/7 \approx 3.142857$. The error $|3.142857 - 3.14159|$ rounds to (4 dp):

problems.prompt: The exact value is $\frac{27}{10} = [2; 1, 2, 3]$. Approximate it by its first convergent $\frac{3}{1}$ and find the approximation error.

steps.instruction: The first convergent of $[2; 1, \dots]$ is $\frac{a_1 a_0 + 1}{a_1} = \frac{1\cdot2 + 1}{1}$. Give it as a fraction.

problems.solutionNarrative: $\frac{27}{10} = 2.7$ and the convergent $\frac{3}{1} = 3$, so the error is $\frac{3}{10} = 0.3$.

problems.prompt: Expand $\frac{5}{2}$ as a continued fraction $[a_0; a_1]$.
Verify the convergent identity $p_k q_{k-1} - p_{k-1} q_k = (-1)^{k+1}$ for $[2; 3, 4]$ at $k = 2$, using $\frac{p_1}{q_1} = \frac{7}{3}$ and $\frac{p_2}{q_2} = \frac{30}{13}$.
problems.solutionNarrative: $p_2 q_1 - p_1 q_2 = 90 - 91 = -1 = (-1)^{3}$, confirming the identity (and that $\gcd(p_2, q_2) = 1$).
$[0; 2] = \frac{1}{2}$ — a leading term of 0 gives a value below 1.

problems.prompt: Expand $\frac{10}{7}$ to a continued fraction, then evaluate it back to confirm you recover $\frac{10}{7}$.
problems.solutionNarrative: $\frac{10}{7} = [1; 2, 3]$, and folding back gives $1 + \frac{3}{7} = \frac{10}{7}$ — a full round trip.
